title: Parametric ReLU (PReLU): Learnable Slopes
concept: Instead of a fixed small slope for negative inputs like Leaky ReLU uses, PReLU lets the network learn the best slope for negative inputs during training, adapting it per neuron.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what does Leaky ReLU do differently from regular ReLU for negative inputs?

options.text: It multiplies them by a small slope instead of zeroing them out
It ignores them completely, same as ReLU

exercises.explain: Right — Leaky ReLU keeps a small trickle alive. But who decides exactly how small that slope should be?

cards.title: Who picks the slope?
cards.body: Leaky ReLU's slope — usually 0.01 — is a number a human picks before training even starts, and it stays fixed forever. But what if the best slope is different for every neuron? What if the network could just figure that out itself?

cards.title: PReLU: a slope the network learns
cards.body: Parametric ReLU (PReLU) works just like Leaky ReLU — small slope for negative inputs, unchanged for positive inputs — except that slope isn't fixed. It's a learnable parameter, adjusted during training the same way weights are, and each neuron can end up with its own personal slope.

cards.title: A dial the network turns itself
cards.body: Leaky ReLU is like a faucet permanently set to a fixed trickle by the plumber before you ever turn it on. PReLU is a faucet with a dial the house learns to adjust on its own — tightening or loosening the trickle for each room (neuron) until it finds the setting that works best.

cards.title: Different neurons, different slopes
cards.body: After training, Neuron A might learn a slope of 0.02 for its negative inputs, while Neuron B — solving a different part of the problem — learns a slope of 0.15. Leaky ReLU would force both neurons to use the exact same fixed slope; PReLU lets each one adapt.

cards.title: Not extra layers — just extra learnable numbers
cards.body: You might assume PReLU makes a network far more complex, since it 'learns' something new. In reality, it only adds one extra learnable number per neuron — the slope — updated by the exact same training process that already adjusts every weight.

left.text:
Slope fixed by a human, e.g. 0.01
Same for every neuron, forever

right.text:
Slope learned during training
Can differ for every neuron

exercises.prompt: What does PReLU learn during training that Leaky ReLU does not?

options.text: The exact slope to use for negative inputs
The number of layers in the network
Whether to use Softmax or Sigmoid

exercises.explain: Right — PReLU treats the negative-input slope as a learnable parameter, not a fixed human choice.

exercises.prompt: True or false: in PReLU, every neuron is forced to use the exact same slope for negative inputs.
exercises.explain: False! PReLU can let each neuron learn its own personal slope.

exercises.sentence: PReLU stands for ___ ReLU, because its slope is a learnable parameter.
exercises.explain: PReLU = Parametric ReLU — 'parametric' because the slope is a learnable parameter.

exercises.prompt: Which activation function adjusts its negative-input slope automatically during training, the same way weights get adjusted?
exercises.explain: PReLU treats the slope as a learnable parameter, updated right alongside the network's weights.

exercises.prompt: Two neurons in the same network handle very different kinds of negative inputs — one needs a gentle slope, the other needs a much steeper one. Regular Leaky ReLU forces both to share one fixed slope. Which activation function solves this, and how?

options.text: PReLU, because it lets each neuron learn its own slope for negative inputs during training instead of using one fixed value for all of them
Softmax, because it distributes values across categories
Regular ReLU, because simplicity always wins
Leaky ReLU, because its fixed slope already adapts automatically

exercises.explain: Exactly! PReLU frees each neuron to learn its own best slope. But straight lines — even learnable ones — aren't the only option for the negative side. Next up: an activation function that curves instead.
